text: Rigid 1,3-Disubstituted Bicyclo[1.1.1]pentanes (BCPs) are widely used as linear bioisoster of para-substituted benzene ring and hence are prone to embed drug candidates with superior pharmacokinetic properties. In order to incorporate BCP structure, so far, the use of the volatile and unstable hydrocarbon, [1.1.1]propellane is inevitable. In addition, the alternatives which are synthesized through other routes do not possess comparable reactivity as that of the parent hydrocarbon. To this end, stable thianthrenium based BCP reagents for the O-, N-, and C-alkylation have been reported for the first time. These reagents are found to be better than the hydrocarbon [1.1.1]propellane and other existing reagents towards the bicyclopentylation reactions. Based on the redox and stereoelectronic properties of the thianthrene core, the reported strain-release method is employed for the synthesis of stable BCP reagents. The labile exocyclic C–S bond in the reagents undergoes selective mesolytic cleavage during single electron reduction of the thianthrenium core to generate the corresponding BCP radicals. These BCP radicals participate in the transition metal mediated bond forming reactions. Finally, late-stage functionalization of various complex structures by using these reagents has been demonstrated with high yields irrespective of diverse and sensitive functional groups present on the substrates.
